mnemonics.title: Pass by Value vs Reference
mnemonics.content:
VALUE MAKES A COPY - CHANGES STAY LOCAL
REFERENCE USES ADDRESS - CHANGES AFFECT ORIGINAL

mnemonics.title: Remember & and *
mnemonics.content:
& AMpersand = ANDDRESS (Address of)
* ASTERisk = ACCESS (Access value at address)

mnemonics.title: Array Passing
mnemonics.content: ARRAYS ALWAYS DECAY - BECOME POINTERS WHEN PASSED

acronyms.usage: Formal parameters are defined in function; Actual parameters are passed during call
Using pointers to achieve pass-by-reference effect in C

visualTips.visualization: Think of a PHOTOCOPY - you have the original document, and the function gets a photocopy. Whatever the function does to the photocopy doesn't affect the original.
Think of a HOUSE KEY - you give the function a key to your house. The function can enter and modify your actual house, not a copy.
Think of a HOTEL REGISTER - you give the desk clerk your ROOM NUMBER (address), not copies of all the rooms. They know where to go to access any room.
Think of a NAME TAG on a BOX - the name tag tells you WHERE the box is (address), while * reads what's INSIDE the box.

analogies.analogy: Like sending a PHOTOCOPY of a document to be edited - the original remains safe and unchanged.
Like giving someone your house keys to move furniture - they can actually change your house, not just a copy.
Like giving someone the ADDRESS of a warehouse - they don't get all the items, just directions to access them.
Like a REMOTE CONTROL to a device - pointing the remote (pointer) at the TV lets you change what's on the screen (value at address).
